one_line_takeaway: RLBF trains LLMs via GRPO with live LLM-critic feedback to emit a lightweight 'backtrack by X tokens' signal that excises mid-generation safety violations without discarding the whole response, cutting attack success rates (e.g., 1-2% vs 14-17% for the paper's own BSAFE+ baseline on standard LMSYS harmful queries, and 4.3-4.7% vs 10.7-13.4% for Circuit Breakers on GCG) while preserving benchmark utility and using far fewer tokens per correction than the prior repeat-and-edit BSAFE mechanism.
key_finding: On LMSYS-MF, baseline IT models had 68-81% ASR; RL alone only marginally reduced this to 61-72%; Circuit Breakers reached 13-18%; BSAFE+ reached 3-6%; and RLBF reached 3-7% (comparable to BSAFE+ on this specific attack). On the standard LMSYS harmful-query subset, however, RLBF substantially outperformed BSAFE+: 1-2% ASR for RLBF versus 14-17% for BSAFE+ (IT: 24-28%, RL: 22-25%, Circuit Breakers: 17-21%). On GCG attacks, RLBF achieved 4.3-4.7% ASR (AdvBench 4.7%, HEx-PHI 4.3%) versus Circuit Breakers 10.7-13.4% and BSAFE+ 5.7-6.6%, with IT/RL baselines at 36.5-65.6%; under an adaptive GCG variant designed to suppress the backtracking tokens, RLBF's ASR was essentially unchanged (4.7% AdvBench, 4.3% HEx-PHI) despite no GCG-style training exposure. On decoding-parameter attacks, RLBF and BSAFE+ were both near-perfect on MaliciousInstruct (1.0% each) with RLBF slightly ahead on HEx-PHI (3.7% vs 5.0%). Utility was preserved: e.g. Gemma2 9B RLBF scored 70.7% MMLU / 35.6% MATH vs IT's 70.6% / 35.4%; LLaMA 3 8B RLBF scored 64.2% BBH / 63.1% GSM8K vs IT's 64.1% / 63.1%. The backtracking ablation (Table 5, LMSYS) showed full RLBF at 1% ASR vs 18% with backtracking disabled entirely and 7% with backtracking disabled only mid-generation, isolating backtracking (not just RL) as the source of the safety gain. A weaker critic (82.0% accuracy) still yielded RLBF at 2-4% ASR on LMSYS versus 14-17% for BSAFE+, indicating gains come mainly from policy learning rather than critic precision. Token efficiency: on triggered corrections, BSAFE+ and RLBF saved an average of 68.3 and 72.6 tokens respectively versus BSAFE's repeat-and-edit format (74.8/79.1 tokens when replayed in BSAFE's format), translating to an estimated 2.5-3.5s saved per correction on hosted models and 5-10s on edge devices.
core_contribution: The paper introduces RL with Backtracking Feedback (RLBF), a two-stage LLM safety framework. Stage 1 is an SFT phase (BSAFE+) that teaches a model to emit a lightweight '[CATEGORY_c] [BACKTRACK_BY_X]' token pair when it detects a safety violation mid-generation, where X is the number of preceding tokens to discard; training data is built by injecting harmful/jailbreak segments into otherwise safe, high-quality responses from a capable base model (rather than generating full responses with a weaker model, as prior BSAFE data did). Stage 2 is a GRPO-based RL phase in which a single LLM safety critic scores entire generated trajectories in real time (rewards: +1.0 safe output or successful correction, -1.0 undetected violation, -0.5 unnecessary backtrack, -0.2 failed/incoherent correction), and the RL loss is combined with an SFT-guidance behavior-cloning term (Ltotal = -J_RL + lambda_SFT * L_SFT_guidance, lambda_SFT=0.1). At inference the backtrack signal is handled by a post-processing/streaming layer that drops the last X tokens from the user-visible stream without reverting the KV cache, avoiding the repeat-and-edit overhead of the prior BSAFE mechanism.
threat_model: The paper targets adversarial and in-distribution safety failures that occur during LLM generation itself, i.e., after decoding has begun, rather than only prompt-level jailbreaks. Attack vectors explicitly evaluated are: (1) Middle Filling (MF) attacks that inject malicious instructions after a benign prefix to exploit 'shallow safety'; (2) Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) adversarial-suffix attacks, including an adaptive GCG variant (Zhang et al. 2025) that also optimizes the suffix to suppress backtracking tokens; (3) decoding-parameter manipulation attacks (e.g., temperature/sampling exploits per Huang et al. 2024); and (4) standard harmful queries from a subset of the LMSYS benchmark. The defender is the model developer training/deploying the LLM; the safety critic is an LLM-based judge used both during RL training and (implicitly) as an evaluation oracle for attack success.
limitations: The paper's own Limitations section (Section 7) states: the computational demands of RLBF, particularly the backtracking process, may challenge some deployment scenarios; and the inherent difficulty of precisely defining 'harmful' content means RLBF's safety protocols may not cover every violation, leaving room for future work on more adaptable policies. Additionally noted in-text: training cost is substantial (~800 GPU-hours/model); the safety critic, while validated at 97.6-98.0% accuracy on a small 100-example hand-built set, is itself an LLM judge used both to shape rewards and (per the paper's framing) to assess attack success, raising a self-evaluation dependency; and RLBF and BSAFE+ perform comparably on MF attacks specifically, so RLBF's largest advantage is on standard harmful queries and GCG/adaptive-GCG rather than uniformly across all attack types.

datasets_used: LMSYS-MF (LMSYS with Middle Filling attack augmentation)
HEx-PHI (GCG and Decoding Parameter attack benchmark)
300k-example augmented SFT dataset built via BSAFE+ violation-injection method
100-example manually constructed critic validation set (43 high/38 medium/19 low severity)
quantitative_results: LMSYS-MF ASR: IT 68-81%, RL 61-72%, Circuit Breakers 13-18%, BSAFE+ 3-6%, RLBF 3-7% (Table 1)
LMSYS ASR: IT 24-28%, RL 22-25%, Circuit Breakers 17-21%, BSAFE+ 14-17%, RLBF 1-2% (Table 1)
GCG ASR on AdvBench: IT 65.6%, RL 62.6%, Circuit Breakers 10.7%, BSAFE+ 6.6%, RLBF 4.7% (Table 2)
GCG ASR on HEx-PHI: IT 36.5%, RL 38.3%, Circuit Breakers 13.4%, BSAFE+ 5.7%, RLBF 4.3% (Table 2)
Decoding Parameter ASR on MaliciousInstruct: IT 84.3%, RL 81.8%, Circuit Breakers 2.0%, BSAFE+ 1.0%, RLBF 1.0% (Table 2)
Decoding Parameter ASR on HEx-PHI: IT 54.9%, RL 51.7%, Circuit Breakers 12.4%, BSAFE+ 5.0%, RLBF 3.7% (Table 2)
Adaptive GCG (suffix optimized to suppress backtracking): RLBF unchanged at 4.7% AdvBench / 4.3% HEx-PHI; BSAFE+ shifts by at most 0.4 points

Per-category attack prevention rate on LMSYS-MF (Gemma2 2B / LLaMA3 1B / LLaMA3 3B): Hate Speech 0.98/0.96/0.96, Toxic 0.96/0.96/0.96, Politics 0.96/0.96/0.98, Health 0.96/0.98/0.96, Dangerous Content 0.94/0.94/0.96, Sexually Explicit 0.92/0.94/0.92, Public Safety 0.94/0.96/0.96, Illicit Drugs 0.94/0.92/0.92, Violent 0.96/0.96/0.96, Finance 0.96/0.96/0.94 (Table 4)
Backtracking ablation on LMSYS: IT 24%, RL 22%, BSAFE+ 14%, RLBF 1%, RLBF w/o backtracking 18%, RLBF w/o backtracking-in-middle 7% (Table 5)
Critic robustness: weaker critic (82.0% accuracy) yields RLBF at 2-4% ASR on LMSYS (vs 1-2% with the stronger critic and 14-17% for BSAFE+); on LMSYS-MF the two critics are comparable at 3-7%
BSAFE-format ASR on identical data: 17-22% ASR on LMSYS (between RL and BSAFE+)

Token savings on triggered corrections: BSAFE+ saves 68.3 tokens, RLBF saves 72.6 tokens over BSAFE's format on average (74.8/79.1 tokens when corrections are replayed in BSAFE's format); estimated 2.5-3.5s saved per correction on hosted models, 5-10s on edge devices

BSAFE data-strategy quality regression noted by paper: IT model trained with BSAFE's data-generation strategy scored 28.2% win rate vs 71.8% for a standard IT model on LMSYS (judged by Gemini 2.0); BSAFE+ narrows this to 49.4% vs 50.6%
baselines_compared: BSAFE (Sel et al., 2025b) — prior work by overlapping authors; RLBF is presented as an advance over it
BSAFE+ — the paper's own improved SFT-only data-generation baseline (no RL stage), used as an ablation/comparison point against full RLBF
